description:
You are a performance-engineer who has just received two raw, space-delimited snapshot files produced by a lightweight profiler.  
They already exist inside your home directory:

• /home/user/profiler/cpu.log  
• /home/user/profiler/mem.log  

Each file contains four non-blank lines: one header line followed by three data lines in identical PID order.  
The layout of both files (single TAB between every field) is:

PID<TAB>Command<TAB>Metric

For /home/user/profiler/cpu.log the Metric column is called “CPU%”.  
For /home/user/profiler/mem.log the Metric column is called “MEM%”.  

Example – cpu.log (including header, real tabs, **no extra spaces**):
PID	Command	CPU%
101	nginx	12.3
202	postgres	43.1
303	redis	5.6

Example – mem.log (including header, real tabs, **no extra spaces**):
PID	Command	MEM%
101	nginx	84
202	postgres	256
303	redis	37

Goal  
Create a new TAB-separated report called /home/user/profiler/combined_profile.tsv that contains the following four columns, in this exact order, with a single TAB between them:

PID	Command	CPU%	MEM%

The header must be on the first line exactly as shown above.  
The three data lines must appear directly underneath, in the same PID order as the source files, and must contain the CPU% values taken from cpu.log and the MEM% values taken from mem.log.

The finished file must therefore look **exactly** like this (where “→” marks a literal tab and is **not** part of the file):

PID→Command→CPU%→MEM%
101→nginx→12.3→84
202→postgres→43.1→256
303→redis→5.6→37

Task  
1. Use only basic command-line utilities that ship with every POSIX-compatible system (e.g. cut, paste, sort, cat, echo).  
2. Do **not** modify the existing cpu.log or mem.log files.  
3. Place the final report at /home/user/profiler/combined_profile.tsv.  
4. After you are confident that the content and formatting match the specification above, list the file to the terminal so the user can visually confirm it.  

Hints (do NOT print them in your final answer – they’re just for you):  
• Two cut commands and one paste command are enough.  
• Remember to supply a header row manually before or after the paste operation.  
• Ensure you use the correct delimiter flag so cut/paste treat the input as TAB-separated.

An automated test will open /home/user/profiler/combined_profile.tsv and compare it byte-for-byte with the expected contents, so pay very close attention to:

• Column order  
• TAB (0x09) as the only delimiter  
• Newline character (0x0A) at the end of every line, including the last one  
• No trailing spaces
truth:
Existing input files BEFORE the agent starts:

1. /home/user/profiler/cpu.log  
Content (each “\t” represents the single TAB byte 0x09, each “\n” the LF byte 0x0A):
PID\tCommand\tCPU%\n
101\tnginx\t12.3\n
202\tpostgres\t43.1\n
303\tredis\t5.6\n

2. /home/user/profiler/mem.log  
Content:
PID\tCommand\tMEM%\n
101\tnginx\t84\n
202\tpostgres\t256\n
303\tredis\t37\n

File that MUST exist after the agent finishes:

/home/user/profiler/combined_profile.tsv  
Exact required content:
PID\tCommand\tCPU%\tMEM%\n
101\tnginx\t12.3\t84\n
202\tpostgres\t43.1\t256\n
303\tredis\t5.6\t37\n
name: task_000000_bcc74fdf
